## ABSTRACT

Large language models have recently been shown to attain reasonable zero-shot generalization on a diverse set of tasks (Brown et al., 2020). It has been hypothesized that this is a consequence of implicit multitask learning in language models’ pretraining (Radford et al., 2019). Can zero-shot generalization instead be directly induced by _explicit_ multitask learning? To test this question at scale, we develop a system for easily mapping any natural language tasks into a human-readable prompted form. We convert a large set of supervised datasets, each with multiple prompts with diverse wording. These prompted datasets allow for benchmarking the ability of a model to perform completely unseen tasks. We fine-tune a pretrained encoder-decoder model (Raffel et al., 2020; Lester et al., 2021) on this multitask mixture covering a wide variety of tasks. The model attains strong zero-shot performance on several standard datasets, often outperforming models up to 16 _×_ its size. Further, our approach attains strong performance on a subset of tasks from the BIG-bench benchmark, outperforming models up to 6 _×_ its size. All trained models are available at https://github.com/bigscience-workshop/t-zero, and all prompts are available at https://github.com/bigscience-workshop/promptsource.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Recent work has shown that large language models exhibit the ability to perform reasonable zeroshot generalization to new tasks (Brown et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021). Despite being trained on only language modeling objectives, these models can perform relatively well at new tasks that they have not been explicitly trained to perform, for instance answering a question on a passage or performing summarization. An influential hypothesis is that large language models generalize to new tasks as a

result of an implicit process of multitask learning (Radford et al., 2019). As a byproduct of learning to predict the next word, a language model is forced to learn from a mixture of implicit tasks included in their pretraining corpus. For example, by training on generic text from a web forum, a model might implicitly learn the format and structure of question answering. This gives large language models the ability to generalize to unseen _tasks_ presented with natural language prompts, going beyond prior multitask studies on generalization to unseen _datasets_ (Khashabi et al., 2020a; Ye et al., 2021). However, this ability requires a sufficiently large model and is sensitive to the wording of its prompts (Perez et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021; Reynolds and McDonell, 2021).

Further, it is an open question how implicit this multitask learning really is. Given the scale of recent language models’ pretraining corpora, it is reasonable to expect that some common natural language processing (NLP) tasks would appear in an explicit form in their pretraining corpora, thereby directly training the models on those tasks. For example, there are many websites that simply contain lists of trivia questions and answers,[1] which are precisely supervised training data for the task of closedbook question answering (Roberts et al., 2020). We hypothesize that such multitask supervision in pretraining plays a large role in zero-shot generalization.

In this paper, we focus on explicitly training language models in a supervised and massively multitask fashion. Our approach uses a training mixture consisting of a large set of different tasks specified in natural language prompts. Our goal is to induce a model to better generalize to unseen tasks without requiring massive scale, as well as being more robust to the wording choices of the prompts. To convert a large set of natural language tasks into prompted form, we use a simple templating language for structured datasets. We develop an interface for prompt collection from public contributors that facilitated the collection of a large multitask mixture with multiple prompts per dataset (Bach et al., 2022). We then train a variant of the T5 encoder-decoder model (Raffel et al., 2020; Lester et al., 2021) on a subset of the tasks (each with multiple datasets) and then evaluate tasks and prompts that the model was _not_ trained on.

Our experiments study two questions. First, does multitask prompted training improve generalization to unseen tasks? Second, does training on a wider range of prompts improve robustness to prompt wording? For the first question, we find that multitask training enables zero-shot task generalization by showing that our model matches or exceeds the performance of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) on 9 out of 11 held-out datasets, despite being about 16 _×_ smaller. We also show that the model improves over a large baseline language model on 13 out of 14 tasks in the BIG-bench benchmark

For the second question, we find that training on more prompts per dataset consistently improves the median and decreases the variability of performance on held-out tasks. Training on prompts from a wider range of datasets also generally improves the median but does not consistently decrease the variability.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this work, we distinguish implicit multitask learning in language model pretraining from _explicit_ multitask learning (Caruana, 1997), the technique for mixing multiple tasks into a single supervised training process. Models trained with multitask learning have long been shown to have improved performance in NLP (Collobert and Weston, 2008). Since different tasks have different outputs, applying multitask learning requires a shared format, and various have been used (Hashimoto et al., 2016; McCann et al., 2018). Several multitask works also explore few-shot and zero-shot generalization to new datasets with large pretrained models (e.g., Vu et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021).

Natural language prompting is the method of reformatting NLP tasks in the format of a natural language response to natural language input. The development of text-to-text pretrained models such as T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) makes prompts a particularly useful method for multitask learning. For example, Khashabi et al. (2020a) reformat 20 question-answering datasets into a single prompt of question: ... (A)... (B)... (C)... context: ..., while later work such as Zhong et al. (2021) and Wang et al. (2021) cast a range of datasets into a single boolean QA prompt or a single NLI prompt, respectively. Although effective, these single-prompt methods typically do not generalize to new prompts or new tasks inexpressible in their fixed format.

More generally, Schick and Sch¨utze (2021) and Brown et al. (2020) popularized using prompts as a generic method for all NLP tasks. Mishra et al. (2021) further extend this approach to a multitask setup, training on prompts for 61 narrowly defined tasks (e.g., question generation, incorrect answer generation) adapted from 9 datasets’ crowdsourcing instructions, whereas we train on and measure generalization across 62 datasets and 12 tasks as traditionally defined in the NLP literature (§3). Additionally, their prompts include labeled examples in addition to instructions, whereas we focus on zero-shot generalization. Lastly, concurrent work by Wei et al. (2021) shares a similar research question with us, although we differ in several substantive regards, e.g., prompt diversity, model scale, and held-out-task scheme. We discuss our differences in detail in Section 7.

Finally, in explaining the success of prompts, the leading hypothesis is that models learn to understand the prompts as task instructions which help them generalize to unseen tasks (Wei et al., 2021; Mishra et al., 2021; Schick and Sch¨utze, 2021; Brown et al., 2020). However, the extent to which this success depends on the semantic meaningfulness of the prompts has been challenged (Webson and Pavlick, 2021; Logan et al., 2021). Thus, in this work, we remain agnostic as to why prompts support generalization. We only claim that prompts serve as a natural format for multitask training which empirically supports generalization to unseen tasks.

## 3 MEASURING GENERALIZATION TO UNSEEN TASKS

We begin by assuming an underlying partition of NLP datasets into tasks. We use the term “task” to refer to a general NLP ability that is tested by a group of specific datasets. To evaluate zero-shot generalization to new tasks, we train on a subset of tasks and evaluate on a held-out group of tasks.

Unfortunately, NLP task categorization is fuzzy, particularly if one tries to isolate a unique skill. For example, many datasets evaluate commonsense knowledge, and some multitask works (e.g., Brown et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2021) define commonsense as a standalone task. However, commonsense datasets differ vastly, ranging from innate knowledge and grade-school science to DIY instructions, US cultural norms, and graduate-level theorems (see Appendix E.1 for a detailed discussion).

Noting that grouping by task is an imperfect heuristic, we err on the side of organizing our task taxonomy according to the task format as opposed to required skill based on conventions in the literature (Khashabi et al., 2020b; Vu et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021). We collect all datasets from these papers and exclude those that are not in English (which also excludes programming languages and structured annotations such as parse trees) or if they require special domain knowledge (e.g., biomedicine). This yields 12 tasks and 62 datasets with publicly contributed prompts in our training

To test zero-shot generalization, we hold out all constituent datasets of four tasks: natural language inference (NLI), coreference resolution, sentence completion, and word sense disambiguation. We choose NLI as a held-out task because humans also zero-shot generalize to NLI as an unseen task: Most humans are never explicitly trained to classify whether a premise sentence entails or contradicts a hypothesis sentence, yet they find it intuitive to perform this task without training (Williams et al., 2020). For the same reason, we also hold out coreference resolution and word sense disambiguation. We further hold out sentence completion because it is a task possibly too similar to NLI (Appendix E.2 discusses this in detail). Additionally, we do not train our main model on any datasets that Brown et al. (2020) used for evaluation, so that our main results will be a fair zero-shot comparison. We also verify that data for those tasks is not leaked through the pretraining corpus (Appendix F).

Lastly, we further evaluate on a subset of the datasets from BIG-bench, which is a recent communitydriven benchmark to create a diverse collection of difficult tasks to test the abilities of large language models. The subset of BIG-bench comprise a language-oriented selection of tasks for which the BIGbench maintainers have prepared preliminary results and which constitute text that is in-vocabulary for the T5 tokenizer (i.e. only contain English-language text without emojis or other special characters). All tasks from BIG-bench are novel tasks that were unseen in our training.

## 4 A UNIFIED PROMPT FORMAT

All datasets are given to our model in natural language prompted form to enable zero-shot experimentation. To facilitate writing a large collection of prompts, we develop a templating language and an application that make it easy to convert diverse datasets into prompts. We define a _prompt_ as consisting of an input template and a target template, along with a collection of associated metadata. The templates are functions mapping a data example into natural language for the input and target sequences. Practically, the templates allow the user to mix arbitrary text with the data fields, metadata, and other code for rendering and formatting raw fields. For example, in the case of an NLI dataset, the example would include fields for Premise, Hypothesis, Label. An input template would be If _{_ Premise _}_ is true, is it also true that _{_ Hypothesis _}_ ?, whereas a target template can be defined with the label choices _{_ Choices[label] _}_ . Here Choices is prompt-specific metadata that consists of the options yes, maybe, no corresponding to label being entailment (0), neutral (1) or contradiction (2). Other metadata documents

To develop prompts, we built an interface for interactively writing prompts on datasets. We put out an open call in the research community for users to contribute prompts. 36 contributors affiliated with 24 institutions in 8 countries participated. Since our goal was to train a model to be robust to prompt format, and since the question of what makes a prompt effective remains unresolved (Webson and Pavlick, 2021; Logan et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021), we encouraged contributors to be open in their style and create a diverse set of prompts. The main annotation guideline was that prompts needed to be grammatical and understandable by a fluent English speaker with no prior experience of the tasks. Additionally, prompts that required explicit counting or numerical indexing were removed in favor of natural language variants. For example, instead of predicting indices of a span extracting answers from a passage, the model is expected to copy the span’s text instead. With these minimal constraints, prompt writers were encouraged to use both formal and creative prompts and various orderings of the data.

Most of the prompts correspond directly to a version of the original proposed task, although we also allow prompts that permuted the original task (for instance, generating a document from its summary). Such non-original-task prompts are included in our training mixtures for improved diversity, but they are not reported in evaluation since they deviate from the metrics and baselines reported by the original datasets.

The details of the prompting language and tool are given in Appendix D and Bach et al. (2022), and the prompts themselves are given in Appendix H. We collected prompts for English datasets, excluding ones that included potentially harmful content or non-natural language such as programming languages. We refer to this collection as the _Public Pool of Prompts_ (P3). As of writing, P3 contains 2073 prompts for 177 datasets (11.7 prompts per dataset on average). Prompts used in experiments are all sourced from P3 except for BIG-bench, the prompts of which are provided by its maintainers.

## 5 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

**Model** At a high level, we a pretrained model on our multi-task training mixture of natural language prompted datasets. Our model uses an encoder-decoder architecture with input text fed to the encoder and target text produced by the decoder. The model is trained to autoregressively generate the target through standard maximum likelihood training. Unlike decoder-only language models such as GPT-3, it is never trained to generate the input.

All models we trained are based on T5, a Transformer-based encoder-decoder language model pretrained with a masked language modeling-style objective on 1T tokens from C4 (Raffel et al., 2020). Since T5’s pretraining objective is generating tokens and only tokens that have been removed from the input text, it is different from the natural text generation format of prompted datasets. Therefore, we use Lester et al. (2021)’s _LM-adapted T5_ model (referred to as T5+LM), produced by training T5 on 100B additional tokens from C4 on a standard language modeling objective.

**Training** Our main model, _T0_ , is trained on the multitask mixture detailed in Section 3 and Table 5. Meanwhile, _T0+_ is the same model with identical hyperparameters except trained on a mixture that adds GPT-3’s evaluation datasets. Lastly, _T0++_ further adds SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019a) to the training mixture (except RTE and CB), which leaves NLI and the BIG-bench tasks as the only held-out tasks.

The above T0 variants are all initialized from the 11B parameters version of T5+LM. To study the effect of scaling and to aid researchers with less resources, we also train _T0 (3B)_ , which has the same training mixture as T0 but is initialized from the 3B parameters version of T5+LM (results reported in Appendix G).

We perform checkpoint selection by choosing the checkpoint that yields the highest score on the validation splits of our training datasets. This still satisfies the _true zero-shot_ (Perez et al., 2021) setting as we do not use any examples from any of the held-out tasks to select the best checkpoint.

ing datasets. This is equivalent to sampling from each dataset in proportion to the number of examples in the dataset. However, the number of examples in each of our training datasets varies by two orders of magnitude. We therefore follow the strategy used in Raffel et al. (2020) and treat any dataset with over 500’000 examples as having 500’000 / num ~~t~~ emplates examples for the purposes of sampling, where num ~~t~~ emplates is the number of templates created for the dataset.

We truncate input and target sequences to 1024 and 256 tokens, respectively. Following Raffel et al. (2020), we use packing to combine multiple training examples into a single sequence to reach the maximum sequence length. We use a batch size of 1024 sequences (corresponding to 2[20] total input tokens per batch) and the Adafactor optimizer (Shazeer and Stern, 2018). Following standard practice for fine-tuning T5, we use a learning rate of 1e-3 and a dropout rate of 0.1.

**Evaluation** We evaluate zero-shot generalization on 11 datasets in 4 held-out traditional NLP tasks: natural language inference, coreference, word sense disambiguation, and sentence completion, as well as 14 novel tasks from BIG-bench (§3). Unless specified otherwise, we report performance on the validation splits. All reported datasets use accuracy as their metric.

## 6 RESULTS

## 6.1 GENERALIZATION TO UNSEEN TASKS

tasks. In Figure 4, we compare T0 against our T5+LM baseline on four held-out tasks. Our approach leads to significant gains over our baseline on all datasets, demonstrating the benefits of multitask prompted training over only language modeling training with an identical model and prompts.

Next, we compare T0 to the zero-shot performance of the largest language models available as of writing, i.e., various GPT-3 models up to 175B parameters. Note that Brown et al. (2020) report performance on a single prompt,[2] whereas we report the median and interquartile range of performance across all prompts in P3 without cherry picking. We find that T0 matches or exceeds the performance of all GPT-3 models on 9 out of 11 held-out datasets. Notably, neither T0 nor GPT-3 is trained on natural language inference, yet T0 outperforms GPT-3 on all NLI datasets, even though our T5+LM baseline does not. The same is true for most datasets of other held-out tasks. The two exceptions are Winogrande and HellaSwag, which we discuss in Section 7.

To evaluate our models on more unseen tasks, we assess the zero-shot performance of T0, T0+, and T0++ on a subset of BIG-bench (BIG-bench collaboration, 2021). Tasks from BIG-bench cover a variety of novel skills not included in our training tasks, such as deducing the order of a sequence of objects, solving logic grid puzzles, and telling apart true statements from common misconceptions. The maintainers of BIG-bench provide a prompt for each dataset, with which we compare our models to a series of preliminary diagnostic baseline models trained by Google and evaluated by the BIG-bench maintainers. ## 6.2 PROMPT ROBUSTNESS

Our second research question is whether training on a wider range of prompts improves robustness to the wording of the prompts. We conduct two ablation experiments on the effects of the average number of prompts per dataset ( _p_ ) and the number of datasets ( _d_ ) used during training.

**Effect of More Prompts per Dataset** In this analysis, we fix _d_ and compare T0 to models with a varying number of prompts per dataset. T0 was trained on some prompts that do not map onto the dataset’s original task, for example “given an answer, generate a plausible question”. Including these prompts results in _p_ being 8 _._ 03 on average (which corresponds to our main T0 model). We compare T0 to models where _p_ = 1 (one randomly chosen original-task prompt per dataset), _p_ = 5 _._ 7 on average (all original-tasks prompts for all datasets), and _p_ = 0 (corresponding to T5+LM without any prompted training). We train all models with the same hyperparameters and the same number of steps. Figure 6 shows that, even with just one prompt per dataset, performance on unseen tasks can improve substantially over the non-prompted baseline, although the spread (interquartile range between Q1 and Q3) does not consistently improve with _p_ = 1. Meanwhile, further increasing _p_ from 1 to an average of 5.7 does yield additional improvement in both median (increases for 8/11 datasets) and spread (decreases for 7/11 datasets). This reinforces our hypothesis that training on more prompts per dataset leads to better and more robust generalization to unseen tasks. Finally, we find that T0’s inclusion all prompts (including those that do not correspond to the dataset’s original task) further improves the median (increases for 9/11 datasets) and spread (decreases for 8/11 datasets), showing that training on non-original-task prompts can also be beneficial.

**Effect of Prompts from More Datasets** In this experiment, we fix _p_ = all available prompts and increase _d_ from 39 to 49 to 55 (T0, T0+, T0++, respectively. See Section 5 for details.) Figure 7 shows that the median performance of all 5 held-out datasets increases as _d_ increases from 39 to 49. However, the spread only decreases for 1 out of 5 datasets. For some datasets (e.g., ANLI), this is an

**Comparing T0 and GPT-3’s robustness** Because Brown et al. (2020) only report one prompt per dataset with no standard deviation, we evaluate GPT-3 via OpenAI’s API[3] on RTE using the same 10 prompts we evaluate T0 in order to estimate GPT-3 robustness’ to different wording of prompts. One of these templates is identical to Brown et al. (2020, p. 59)’s reported prompt, which scores an accuracy of 58.8%, lower than the 63.5% reported in Brown et al. (2020). ## 7 COMPARISON TO FLAN

Concurrent to our work, Wei et al. (2021) proposes _FLAN_ , which shares largely the same method of enabling zero-shot generalization through multitask prompted training. With a mixture of datasets similar to ours, they train multiple decoder-only language models, each with a single held-out task (cf. we focus on training one model with multiple held-out tasks in order to evaluate the model’s ability to generalize to diverse tasks.) Compared to FLAN, T0’s zero-shot performance is better on CB and RTE, similar on Story Cloze and COPA, and worse on Winogrande and ANLI. T0++ outperforms FLAN on CB, RTE, and COPA and matches FLAN’s performance on Winogrande and ANLI. Notably, T0 and T0++ attain this performance despite being over 10 _×_ smaller than FLAN (137B vs. 11B parameters). Appendix B discusses our differences in details.

## 8 CONCLUSION

We demonstrate that multitask prompted training can enable strong zero-shot generalization abilities in language models. This approach provides an effective alternative to unsupervised language model pretraining, often enabling our T0 model to outperform models many times its size. We also perform ablation studies demonstrating the importance of including many diverse prompts and the impact of increasing the number of datasets in each task. To enable future work on improving zero-shot generalization, we release all models trained in this paper in addition to the collection of prompts we created and our prompt annotation tool.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a compelling and highly influential study on the efficacy of explicit multitask prompted training for zero-shot task generalization. The core strength of the work lies in its systematic approach to converting supervised datasets into a "prompted" format, effectively bridging the gap between traditional supervised learning and the flexible, instruction-following capabilities of large-scale autoregressive models like GPT-3. The clarity of the experimental design—specifically the decision to evaluate on entirely "held-out" task categories—rigorously tests the hypothesis that explicit multitask supervision can substitute for the massive parameter scale typically required for zero-shot performance. By achieving state-of-the-art results with a model 16× smaller than GPT-3, the authors offer a more compute-efficient path toward general-purpose NLP tools.

The most significant issue, which is inherent to this methodology but requires careful scrutiny, is the potential for "task leakage." Given the breadth of the training mixture, the distinction between a "seen" and "unseen" task can become blurred; for instance, if the model is trained on several types of classification and inference, its generalization to a specific new NLI dataset may be more indicative of sophisticated pattern matching than true zero-shot reasoning. Additionally, while the paper highlights improved robustness to prompt phrasing, it remains to be seen how the model handles highly adversarial or non-canonical prompts that fall outside the distribution of the "human-readable" templates used during training. Overall, however, the paper is exceptionally well-structured, the collaborative "BigScience" framework for prompt collection is a significant contribution to the community, and the results fundamentally shift our understanding of the relationship between scale and supervision.

comments.title: Classification of Target Response as 'Zero-Shot' in the presence of Supervised Multi-tasking
comments.quote: Our goal is to induce a model to better generalize to unseen tasks without requiring massive scale... After training on a diverse mixture of tasks (top), our model is evaluated on zero-shot generalization to tasks that are not seen during training (bottom).
comments.explanation: The authors define their evaluation as 'zero-shot generalization.' However, because the model is explicitly fine-tuned on a massive supervised mixture (P3) to learn prompted instructions, this differs from the prompt-only 'zero-shot' definition used for models like GPT-3. While the authors distinguish between 'implicit' and 'explicit' multitask learning, the claim of 'zero-shot' performance might benefit from a more explicit qualifier (e.g., 'zero-shot task transfer') to avoid confusion with models that have seen no supervised data. This conceptual ambiguity also relates to the 'Ambiguity in the scale comparison with GPT-3' and 'Ambiguity in True Zero-Shot Checkpoint Selection' issues, as the level of 'supervision' vs 'zero-shot' behavior is the central logical tension of the paper.

comments.title: Reproducibility and specification of evaluation subsets
comments.quote: The subset of BIG-bench comprise a language-oriented selection of tasks for which the BIG-bench maintainers have prepared preliminary results...
comments.explanation: The specific composition of evaluation sets is underspecified. For BIG-bench, Section 3 does not explicitly list which 14 tasks were selected, and Figure 2 only lists examples. This makes it difficult to replicate the 6x efficiency claim precisely. Similarly, for classification tasks like NLI, the text does not clarify if evaluation is performed via free generation or the more common (and higher-performing) constrained log-likelihood ranking of specific 'Choices' metadata. These methodological gaps hinder exact reproduction of the reported benchmark scores.

comments.title: Potentially circular justification for task selection
comments.quote: We choose NLI as a held-out task because humans also zero-shot generalize to NLI as an unseen task...
comments.explanation: The authors justify holding out NLI by claiming it is 'intuitive' for humans. However, if a task is so fundamental to reasoning that it is ubiquitous in human thought, it is highly likely that the underlying logic is prevalent in the 1T tokens of pretraining data (C4). This creates a logical risk that the evaluation is measuring the retrieval of implicit pretraining supervision rather than generalization to truly 'novel' tasks, potentially confounding the 'Supervised Multitask Hypothesis.'

comments.title: Vagueness in task taxonomy and exclusion criteria
comments.quote: We collect all datasets from these papers and exclude those that are not in English [...] or if they require special domain knowledge (e.g., biomedicine).
comments.explanation: The definition of a 'task' versus a 'dataset' is central to the paper's claims, but the authors admit their categorization is a 'fuzzy' heuristic. Furthermore, it is unclear if exclusions (like 'special domain knowledge') apply only to training or also the evaluation set. If difficult domain-specific tasks were excluded from evaluation, it might artificially simplify the zero-shot challenge, making the boundary conditions of the primary claim underspecified.

comments.title: Missing verb in model description
comments.quote: **Model** At a high level, we a pretrained model on our multi-task training mixture of natural language prompted datasets.
comments.explanation: There is a missing verb in the first sentence of the 'Model' subsection (likely 'fine-tune' or 'train'). This is the primary definition of the experimental procedure; the absence of the specific action (fine-tuning vs. freezing) momentarily obscures the central methodology.

comments.title: Potential numerical and notation inconsistencies
comments.quote: treat any dataset with over 500’000 examples as having 500’000 / num ~~t~~ emplates examples... In this analysis, we fix _d_ and compare T0 to models with a varying number of prompts _p_...
comments.explanation: The text contains several minor notation issues: 1) The sampling equation appears to have an OCR artifact ('~~t~~') and uses logic that might penalize datasets with more prompts, which is a critical reproduction detail. 2) The variables 'd' (datasets) and 'p' (prompts) are used in the ablation analysis before being formally defined. 3) The text oscillates between '16x smaller' (vs GPT-3 175B) and '6x smaller' (referring to specific BIG-bench baselines), which could confuse a reader about the consistent scale of the model.

comments.title: Potential bias in Rank Classification and Robustness results
comments.quote: For simplicity, we do not apply length normalization... One of these templates is identical to Brown et al. (2020, p. 59)’s reported prompt, which scores an accuracy of 58.8%, lower than the 63.5% reported.
comments.explanation: The evaluation methodology may introduce biases in comparisons. The lack of length normalization in log-likelihood ranking typically penalizes longer completions, which may explain weaker performance on HellaSwag. Additionally, the robustness comparison with GPT-3 relies on a reproduction that underperformed the original reported results; without clarification on API parameters (like null-input normalization), it is unclear if T0’s claimed superior robustness is due to training or differences in the evaluation strategy.

comments.title: Bibliographic Errors (OCR and Attribution)
comments.quote: Qiang Ning Ben Zhou... doi: 10.1162/tacl ~~a 0~~ 0338.
comments.explanation: The bibliography contains technical errors: 'Qiang Ning Ben Zhou' is a concatenation of two distinct authors (Qiang Ning and Ben Zhou), and the DOI for the Bartolo et al. (2020) reference contains OCR artifacts that render the URL/identifier broken.

progressive_original__gemini-3-flash-pre.overall_feedback:

The core strength of the work lies in its systematic approach to converting supervised datasets into a "prompted" format, effectively bridging the gap between traditional supervised learning and the flexible, instruction-following capabilities of large-scale autoregressive models like GPT-3. The clarity of the experimental design—specifically the decision to evaluate on entirely "held-out" task categories—rigorously tests the hypothesis that explicit multitask supervision can substitute for the massive parameter scale typically required for zero-shot performance. By achieving state-of-the-art results with a model 16× smaller than GPT-3, the authors offer a more compute-efficient path toward general-purpose NLP tools.

The most significant issue, which is inherent to this methodology but requires careful scrutiny, is the potential for "task leakage." Given the breadth of the training mixture, the distinction between a "seen" and "unseen" task can become blurred; for instance, if the model is trained on several types of classification and inference, its generalization to a specific new NLI dataset may be more indicative of sophisticated pattern matching than true zero-shot reasoning. Additionally, while the paper highlights improved robustness to prompt phrasing, it remains to be seen how the model handles highly adversarial or non-canonical prompts that fall outside the distribution of the "human-readable" templates used during training. Overall, however, the paper is exceptionally well-structured, the collaborative "BigScience" framework for prompt collection is a significant contribution to the community, and the results fundamentally shift our understanding of the relationship between scale and supervision.

comments.title: Classification of Target Response as 'Zero-Shot' in the presence of Supervised Multi-tasking
comments.quote: Our goal is to induce a model to better generalize to unseen tasks without requiring massive scale... After training on a diverse mixture of tasks (top), our model is evaluated on zero-shot generalization to tasks that are not seen during training (bottom).
comments.explanation: The authors define their evaluation as 'zero-shot generalization.' Traditionally, in the context of GPT-3 (Brown et al. 2020), 'zero-shot' refers to a model's performance without any gradient updates on the task-specific data. However, since T0 is explicitly fine-tuned on a massive supervised mixture (P3) to learn the 'prompted form,' the term 'zero-shot' here essentially refers to 'held-out task generalization.' While Figure 1 and Section 3 clearly explain that the *tasks* are unseen, labeling this 'zero-shot' could be misunderstood by a reader expecting a model that hasn't seen any supervised instructional data. The authors correctly distinguish their approach from 'implicit' multitask learning, but the claim of 'zero-shot' performance might benefit from a more explicit qualifier (e.g., 'zero-shot task transfer') to avoid confusion with pre-training-only models.

comments.title: Reproducibility of the BIG-bench subset evaluation
comments.quote: The subset of BIG-bench comprise a language-oriented selection of tasks for which the BIG-bench maintainers have prepared preliminary results and which constitute text that is in-vocabulary for the T5 tokenizer
comments.explanation: The authors state they evaluate on a 'subset' of 14 tasks from BIG-bench (later cited as 13 out of 14 improvements). However, Section 3 does not explicitly list the names of these 14 tasks, and Figure 2 shows a 'BIG-Bench' category with several entries (like 'Strategy QA', 'Syllogisms', 'Known Unknowns'), but it's not immediately clear if these are the exact 14 datasets used for the primary comparison or if they are just examples. Given that BIG-bench is a massive, evolving community benchmark, the specific selection of these 14 datasets and the specific versions of the prompts used (provided by maintainers) are critical for reproducing the reported 6x efficiency claim. While the code is on GitHub, the text itself is underspecified regarding the exact composition of this evaluation subset.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the scale comparison with GPT-3
comments.quote: The model attains strong zero-shot performance on several standard datasets, often outperforming models up to 16 × its size.
comments.explanation: The paper compares the 11B parameter T0 model to the 175B parameter GPT-3 (which is roughly 16x larger). However, the comparison is between a model that has undergone supervised multitask fine-tuning (T0) and a model that is performing zero-shot inference via its pre-training objective only (GPT-3). While the authors acknowledge their goal is to show that explicit training can overcome the need for scale, a reader might be misled regarding the 'efficiency' if they don't account for the fact that T0 had access to thousands of supervised prompt examples per training dataset (P3). The claim is technically supported, but the comparison involves different levels of supervision during the fine-tuning/alignment phase, not just a difference in parameter scale.

comments.title: Potentially circular justification for holding out NLI
comments.quote: We choose NLI as a held-out task because humans also zero-shot generalize to NLI as an unseen task: Most humans are never explicitly trained to classify whether a premise sentence entails or contradicts a hypothesis sentence, yet they find it intuitive to perform this task without training (Williams et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The authors justify holding out Natural Language Inference (NLI) by claiming it is a task humans naturally 'zero-shot.' While this is an interesting intuition, it borders on circular or at least logically shaky for a rigorous machine learning evaluation. In Section 1, the authors acknowledge the hypothesis that LMs generalize because they find 'implicit tasks' in their pretraining corpora. By choosing a task for evaluation that is 'intuitive' or ubiquitous in human reasoning, they may be selecting for the very thing they identify as a confounding variable in the 'Supervised Multitask Hypothesis': the presence of implicit supervision in the pretraining data (C4). If NLI is so fundamental that humans 'find it intuitive,' it is highly likely that the underlying logic is prevalent in the 1T tokens of pretraining data, potentially making it a less-than-ideal candidate for measuring generalization to 'novel' tasks compared to more arbitrary or specialized tasks. They do mention a leakage check in Appendix F, which partially mitigates this, but the logical framing here slightly overstates the purity of the 'unseen' nature of NLI logic.

comments.title: Vagueness in task taxonomy and exclusion criteria
comments.quote: We collect all datasets from these papers and exclude those that are not in English [...] or if they require special domain knowledge (e.g., biomedicine).
comments.explanation: The authors aim to measure generalization to 'unseen tasks,' but the definition of what constitutes a 'task' versus a 'dataset' is central to their claims. They admit the categorization is a 'fuzzy' and 'imperfect heuristic,' yet they use it as the primary boundary for their experimental control. The exclusion of 'special domain knowledge' like biomedicine is noted, but it isn't specified whether this exclusion applies only to the training set or also the evaluation set. If such tasks were excluded from the evaluation as well, it might simplify the 'zero-shot' challenge, as domain-specific tasks often represent the most difficult form of zero-shot generalization. For a reader trying to replicate the 'task' versus 'dataset' split, the lack of a formal rubric for what defines a 'task' (beyond citing past conventions) makes the boundary conditions of their primary claim somewhat underspecified.

comments.title: Missing verb in model description
comments.quote: **Model** At a high level, we a pretrained model on our multi-task training mixture of natural language prompted datasets.
comments.explanation: Looking at the 'Model' subsection under Section 5, there is a missing verb in the first sentence (likely 'fine-tune' or 'train'). While the general intent is clear from the context of the paper summary and the following sentences describing the encoder-decoder architecture, this is the primary definition of the experimental procedure. Given that the paper distinguishes between 'implicit' and 'explicit' multitask learning, the specific action taken on the pretrained model (e.g., fine-tuning vs. freezing with prompt tuning) is a central methodological detail that is momentarily lost here, though later sentences imply full fine-tuning.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'target template' definition for classification tasks
comments.quote: whereas a target template can be defined with the label choices _{_ Choices[label] _}_ . Here Choices is prompt-specific metadata that consists of the options yes, maybe, no corresponding to label being entailment (0), neutral (1) or contradiction (2).
comments.explanation: The authors describe a system where the target is a natural language string. However, they do not explicitly state how evaluation is performed for multi-choice or classification tasks during inference. If the model is 'trained to autoregressively generate the target', a reader might wonder if evaluation is done via free generation (which could produce 'Yes' or 'yes' or 'Indeed') or by ranking the log-likelihood of the specific strings in the 'Choices' metadata. While Figure 3 shows the templates, the 'Experimental Setup' does not clarify if the zero-shot evaluation on tasks like NLI uses constrained log-likelihood ranking or greedy generation. This is a key methodological detail for 'zero-shot' benchmarking reproducibility, as ranking known choices typically yields higher performance than free generation for encoder-decoder models.

comments.title: Potential numerical inconsistency in model size comparison
comments.quote: T0 matches or exceeds the zero-shot performance of GPT-3 on 9 out of 11 held-out datasets despite being 16× smaller.
comments.explanation: The paper summary and Section 1 claim T0 is 16x smaller than GPT-3. The caption for Figure 4 shows T0 and T5+LM at 11B parameters. GPT-3's largest version is 175B parameters. 175 / 11 is approximately 15.9, which rounds to 16x. However, the text also mentions 'outperforming models up to 6x its size' regarding BIG-bench. Later in the text of Section 5, it mentions initialized from '11B parameters version of T5+LM' and also a '3B parameters version'. If the 11B model is compared to GPT-3 175B, the 16x math holds. I should ensure the reader isn't confused by the 6x and 16x figures appearing near each other; however, the authors specify the 6x refers to BIG-bench models specifically (likely referring to GPT-3 6.7B or similar baselines used in that specific benchmark), so it is consistent but requires careful reading.

comments.title: Possible OCR Error in Sampling Frequency Equation
comments.quote: treat any dataset with over 500’000 examples as having 500’000 / num ~~t~~ emplates examples for the purposes of sampling
comments.explanation: The text describes a heuristic for balancing datasets during multitask training. It states that large datasets are capped at 500,000 examples, but then divides this cap by 'num ~~t~~ emplates'. If 'num ~~t~~ emplates' refers to the number of prompt templates for that dataset, this logic seems counter-intuitive. Usually, in multitask prompted training (like that described in Raffel et al. 2020 or Wei et al. 2021), the goal is to ensure the model sees a diverse range of data. If a dataset has more templates, dividing the total allowed examples by the number of templates actually reduces the representation of that dataset's underlying data relative to others. However, looking at the OCR artifacts (~~t~~), it is highly likely the intended notation was 'num_templates'. If the authors intended to cap the total prompted instances (examples × templates) at 500,000, the math holds. But if they intended to cap the raw examples at 500,000 regardless of templates (to avoid a high-prompt-count dataset dominating the mixture), then dividing by the template count is a specific hyperparameter choice that effectively penalizes datasets with more crowdsourced prompts. Given the claim that 'increasing the number of prompts per dataset improves performance', this sampling reduction for high-prompt datasets is a critical reproduction detail that feels slightly underspecified or mathematically obscured by the OCR.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'True Zero-Shot' Checkpoint Selection
comments.quote: We perform checkpoint selection by choosing the checkpoint that yields the highest score on the validation splits of our training datasets. This still satisfies the _true zero-shot_ (Perez et al., 2021) setting as we do not use any examples from any of the held-out tasks to select the best checkpoint.
comments.explanation: The authors claim this satisfies the 'true zero-shot' definition by Perez et al. (2021). However, Perez et al. emphasize that using a validation set—even from 'seen' tasks—to pick a checkpoint for 'unseen' tasks can still lead to over-optimistic results if the model's training dynamics on seen tasks correlate strongly with performance on held-out tasks. While the authors are transparent about using the training-task validation sets, the claim that this *is* 'true zero-shot' is a matter of terminological debate in the field. A stricter 'true zero-shot' would use a fixed number of steps or a heuristic independent of any validation performance. This is a framing concern regarding whether the selection process introduces a 'cross-task' leakage in terms of hyperparameter/checkpoint tuning.

comments.title: Potential Missing Normalization in Rank Classification
comments.quote: For simplicity, we do not apply length normalization to the log-likelihoods of the target options.
comments.explanation: In zero-shot evaluation via rank classification, models typically have a bias toward shorter strings because log-likelihoods (sums of log probabilities) are naturally more negative for longer sequences. Standard practice (as noted in Brown et al. 2020 or Holtzman et al. 2021) often involves normalizing by the number of tokens to level the playing field between options like 'Yes' and 'It is highly likely'. While the authors acknowledge this choice 'for simplicity,' it may explain the weaker performance on tasks like HellaSwag or Winogrande (where completions vary in length and complex nuances), which are cited as exceptions to T0's success. This choice might disadvantage the model in specific benchmarks compared to GPT-3, which does use various normalization techniques.

comments.title: Unexplained variables 'd' and 'p' in ablation analysis
comments.quote: In this analysis, we fix _d_ and compare T0 to models with a varying number of prompts per dataset. ... Including these prompts results in _p_ being 8 _._ 03 on average ... we fix _p_ = all available prompts and increase _d_ from 39 to 49 to 55
comments.explanation: In Section 6 (Ablations), the authors introduce the variables 'd' and 'p' to represent the number of datasets and the number of prompts per dataset, respectively. While a reader can eventually infer their meanings from the context (e.g., 'd' shifts from 39 to 55 as the model moves from T0 to T0++), these are never formally defined or introduced prior to their usage in the formulas/descriptions. This creates initial confusion when the text states 'we fix d' without having established what 'd' stands for in the notation summary or the Experimental Setup. A simple parenthetical definition (e.g., 'd datasets' and 'p prompts') upon first use would resolve this.

comments.title: Ambiguity in GPT-3 robustness comparison
comments.quote: One of these templates is identical to Brown et al. (2020, p. 59)’s reported prompt, which scores an accuracy of 58.8%, lower than the 63.5% reported in Brown et al. (2020).
comments.explanation: The authors compare T0's robustness to GPT-3 by evaluating GPT-3 on 10 prompts. They note that their reproduction of the official GPT-3 prompt yielded 58.8% vs the original paper's 63.5%. While they correctly identify that 'davinci' is likely the 175B model, the discrepancy in results—and the subsequent claim that GPT-3's performance was 'roughly random-guessing' on the other 9 prompts—depends heavily on the specific API parameters used (e.g., temperature, top_p, and crucially, whether they used the log-likelihood 'Rank Classification' method mentioned in the summary or greedy generation). Since the paper claims to use Rank Classification for T0, but doesn't explicitly confirm the exact API implementation details for these GPT-3 robustness runs (like whether null-input normalization was used, which significantly affects GPT-3's zero-shot stability), the conclusion that T0 is inherently more robust might be confounded by the evaluation strategy rather than the model's training.

comments.title: Metric-specific prompt exclusion in evaluations
comments.quote: Such non-original-task prompts are included in our training mixtures for improved diversity, but they are not reported in evaluation since they deviate from the metrics and baselines reported by the original datasets.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that training on more prompts per dataset (p) improves robustness and median performance. However, there is a potential 'evaluation-selection' bias here. If the 'non-original-task' prompts (like generating a question from an answer) are excluded from the evaluation sets because they don't fit the standard metrics, the evaluation of 'robustness' is restricted only to the subset of prompts that look like the original task. While this is a reasonable decision to maintain comparability with GPT-3, it means the claim about robustness only applies to 'standard task formulations' rather than the full diversity of prompts the model was trained on. The context mostly resolves this by being transparent about the exclusion, but it slightly limits the scope of the claim that 'T0 is more robust to the wording choices of the prompts'.

comments.title: Author Name Anomaly in Bibliography
comments.quote: Qiang Ning Ben Zhou, Daniel Khashabi and Dan Roth. “going on a vacation” takes longer than “going for a walk”: A study of temporal commonsense understanding. In EMNLP , 2019.
comments.explanation: Looking at this citation, the author string 'Qiang Ning Ben Zhou' appears to be a concatenation error or an OCR misread of two distinct authors: Qiang Ning and Ben Zhou. The actual paper '“Going on a vacation” takes longer than “going for a walk”: A study of temporal commonsense understanding' is authored by Ben Zhou, Qiang Ning, Daniel Khashabi, and Dan Roth. While this could be an OCR artifact where punctuation was lost, it currently reads as a single name which could lead to incorrect attribution in citation indices. However, the identity of the paper remains clear from the title and year.

comments.title: Potential Pseudonym in Citation
comments.quote: Emily M. Bender, Timnit Gebru, Angelina McMillan-Major, and Shmargaret Shmitchell. On the dangers of stochastic parrots: Can language models be too big?
comments.explanation: The author list for the 'Stochastic Parrots' paper includes 'Shmargaret Shmitchell'. In the context of the field, it is well-known that this is a pseudonym (used by Margaret Mitchell during a period of corporate dispute). In a formal academic paper, a reviewer might wonder if this was an OCR error for 'Margaret Mitchell'. However, the authors of the current paper are correctly citing the work as it was officially published in the FAccT '21 proceedings. Therefore, this is a correct representation of the source material and not an error by the authors or the OCR.

comments.title: Incomplete Metadata in OCR
comments.explanation: The OCR has introduced '~~a 0~~' into the DOI and URL for the Bartolo et al. (2020) reference. This likely represents an error in processing the specific character string 'a_00338' which is common in TACL indexing. A reader trying to follow this link directly would find it broken. While the intended string 'a_00338' is highly probable, the current formatting renders the identifier technically incorrect.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper effectively introduces a compelling and well-motivated research question, successfully bridging the gap between implicit multitask learning observed in large language models and the potential benefits of explicit, instruction-tuned training. The authors bring significant clarity to the problem space by proposing a clear, replicable framework for prompt-based multitask learning, supported by a robust methodological design that leverages encoder-decoder architectures (T5) to achieve impressive performance efficiency. The writing style is professional and concise, adhering to the high standards expected for conference publication. The most significant strength is the empirical demonstration that models significantly smaller than GPT-3 can achieve superior zero-shot generalization through this approach. The primary challenge for the authors moving forward is to ensure that the empirical findings—specifically regarding the "robustness to prompt wording"—are scrutinized against potential data contamination, as the reliance on an extensive multitask mixture necessitates a rigorous evaluation of whether held-out tasks possess subtle overlaps with the training distribution. Overall, the foundational setup and technical approach are exemplary, providing a clear trajectory for advancements in zero-shot task generalization.

comments.title: Ambiguity in '16x smaller' claim regarding GPT-3
comments.quote: our model matches or exceeds the performance of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) on 9 out of 11 held-out datasets, despite being about 16 	imes smaller.
comments.explanation: The '16x smaller' claim is contextually misleading. It identifies the model as 16x smaller than the GPT-3 175B variant, but ignores that other GPT-3 baselines (6.7B and 13B) are smaller than the 11B T0 model. The paper should clarify this comparison to avoid implying a universal size advantage across all reported baselines.

comments.title: Ambiguity in '12 tasks and 62 datasets' count
comments.quote: This yields 12 tasks and 62 datasets with publicly contributed prompts in our training and evaluation mixtures (Figure 2) as of writing.
comments.explanation: There is a mathematical inconsistency between the stated count (62 datasets) and the visual list in Figure 2, which appears to contain significantly more unique labels. Without a mapping or corrected count, the text is ambiguous and difficult to reconcile with the provided figure.

comments.title: Potential overclaim regarding 'fair' zero-shot comparison
comments.quote: Additionally, we do not train our main model on any datasets that Brown et al. (2020) used for evaluation, so that our main results will be a fair zero-shot comparison.
comments.explanation: The claim of a 'fair' zero-shot comparison is misleading. The T0 model undergoes supervised multitask fine-tuning on a wide array of datasets that provide task-specific knowledge, which is fundamentally distinct from the base-model zero-shot evaluation described in Brown et al. (2020).

comments.title: Methodological misidentification of training process
comments.quote: At a high level, we a pretrained model on our multi-task training mixture of natural language prompted datasets.
comments.explanation: The sentence contains a grammatical error (missing verb) and incorrectly identifies the core contribution as a 'pretrained' model. The study involves supervised fine-tuning of a T5 model, not base-level pretraining on these mixtures.

comments.explanation: The paper contains formatting errors in mathematical notation, specifically extraneous artifacts ('num ~~t~~ emplates') and malformed exponents ('2[20]' instead of '2^20'). These interfere with the readability of technical definitions.

comments.explanation: The paper claims an 'about 16x smaller' scale compared to GPT-3. Given that the main model (T0) is 11B parameters, 16x smaller would put the comparison at ~176B parameters (GPT-3 175B). However, Figure 4 shows results for GPT-3 versions at 6.7B, 13B, and 175B. While the paper identifies '16x smaller' as a general comparison to the state-of-the-art 175B model, it could be misleading to suggest this throughout the text without clarifying that other GPT-3 baselines (like 6.7B and 13B) are actually smaller than their 11B model. It is a minor logical issue that simplifies the headline comparison at the expense of accuracy across all reported baselines.

comments.title: Potentially circular reasoning regarding 'Implicit Multitask Learning'
comments.quote: An influential hypothesis is that large language models generalize to new tasks as a result of an implicit process of multitask learning (Radford et al., 2019). ... It is an open question how implicit this multitask learning really is. ... We hypothesize that such multitask supervision in pretraining plays a large role in zero-shot generalization.
comments.explanation: The authors posit that existing large language model success is due to 'implicit multitask learning' found in pretraining corpora (e.g., trivia websites). They then 'test' this by explicitly prompting models. While the framing is clear, there is a risk of a logical conflation: if they characterize the web-crawled pretraining data as 'multitask supervision,' then the distinction between 'implicit' and 'explicit' multitask learning becomes a matter of degree rather than a fundamental difference in methodology. The paper does a good job of differentiating them, but the justification for why this specific experiment confirms the *cause* of the original zero-shot generalization (rather than just demonstrating a better way to do it) remains slightly underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in '12 tasks and 62 datasets' count
comments.quote: This yields 12 tasks and 62 datasets with publicly contributed prompts in our training and evaluation mixtures (Figure 2) as of writing.
comments.explanation: The text states the study uses '12 tasks and 62 datasets.' Looking at Figure 2, the grid lists 5 categories (Multiple-Choice QA, Closed-Book QA, Structure-To-Text, Sentence Completion, BIG-Bench) with many sub-tasks underneath. Summing the items provided in the list for all columns exceeds 62 significantly. While it is possible some listed items constitute 'datasets' that haven't been fully included or that the authors use a specific taxonomy where many items map to one 'task,' the inconsistency between the count (62) and the visual list (which appears to contain upwards of 80 unique labels) is ambiguous. Without a clear mapping, a reader cannot reconcile the text with the provided figure.

comments.title: Potential overclaim regarding 'fair' zero-shot comparison
comments.quote: Additionally, we do not train our main model on any datasets that Brown et al. (2020) used for evaluation, so that our main results will be a fair zero-shot comparison.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that not training on the exact evaluation datasets used by Brown et al. (2020) ensures a 'fair zero-shot comparison.' However, the T0 model is trained on a massive mixture of 62 datasets (many of which are linguistic or QA-based tasks). Even if the specific evaluation datasets (e.g., LAMBADA or BoolQ as used in GPT-3) are held out, the model has been exposed to a wealth of similar tasks. This is arguably not a 'fair' comparison in a strict zero-shot sense, as the model is 'fine-tuned on a multitask mixture,' whereas the GPT-3 evaluation is on a base language model. The authors acknowledge this elsewhere, but the claim that this specific exclusion makes it a 'fair' zero-shot comparison in the same vein as GPT-3's evaluation is potentially misleading to readers unfamiliar with the distinction between base-model zero-shot and multitask-fine-tuned zero-shot.

comments.title: Grammatical error in the first sentence of the Training section
comments.quote: At a high level, we a pretrained model on our multi-task training mixture of natural language prompted datasets.
comments.explanation: The sentence is missing a verb (e.g., 'train'). While the intent is clear given the subsequent sentences, it is a significant typographical omission in the methodology description of the primary model.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for model training
comments.quote: At a high level, we a pretrained model on our multi-task training mixture of natural language prompted datasets.
comments.explanation: The text states the model is 'a pretrained model,' but the core contribution described in the paper is the *fine-tuning* process of a T5 model on the multitask mixture. Using 'pretrained' here is technically misleading regarding the methodology, as it implies the model was trained from scratch or using unsupervised pretraining on these tasks, rather than supervised fine-tuning.

comments.title: OCR-induced character errors in formula description
comments.quote: 500’000 / num ~~t~~ emplates
comments.explanation: In the paragraph describing the dataset sampling strategy, the characters 'num ~t~ emplates' contain extraneous tilde/strike-through artifacts (likely remnants of the OCR process misreading 'num_templates'). While a reader will correctly infer this means 'number of templates', it appears as a garbled variable name in the middle of a mathematical description. Given the instructions to flag symbols inconsistent with surrounding usage if they pose a real issue, this is a minor technical oversight.

comments.title: Potential numerical discrepancy in batch size description
comments.quote: 1024 sequences (corresponding to 2[20] total input tokens per batch)
comments.explanation: The text states '2[20] total input tokens per batch'. Given that 1024 sequences are used, if each sequence has a length up to 1024 (as defined in the same paragraph), the total tokens per batch would be 1024 * 1024 = 1,048,576. 2^20 is exactly 1,048,576. The notation '2[20]' is clearly a typo for 2^20 (or 2^{20}). The brackets might be an OCR misread or a formatting error. While the math is correct, the notation is ambiguous/malformed.

comments.title: OCR Artifacts in DOI Presentation
comments.explanation: The OCR process has introduced a stray '~~a 0~~' sequence in the DOI and URL provided for the Bartolo et al. (2020) reference (e.g., 'tacl a 0 0338' instead of the standard 'tacl_a_00338'). While I acknowledge that the prompt instructions explicitly warn that 'some notation errors may remain' due to OCR, the inclusion of these artifacts in a formal bibliography can mislead a reader attempting to verify the source. Given that the underlying citation is correct and the artifact is clearly a scanning error, this is a minor issue.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated investigation into whether explicit multitask prompted training can induce zero-shot generalization in language models, as opposed to relying on implicit multitask learning during pretraining. The clarity of the exposition is strong: the research question is precisely stated, the methodology (converting tasks into prompted form, training T0 on a diverse mixture) is clearly described, and the results—outperforming GPT-3 on 9/11 held-out tasks despite being 16× smaller—are impressive and effectively communicated. The figure and abstract help ground the reader in the approach. However, several significant issues merit attention. First, the claim of “zero-shot” generalization is nuanced: while the model has not seen the specific held-out tasks during training, the massive multitask mixture may teach a general “prompt-following” ability that borders on meta-learning, potentially diluting the conventional zero-shot interpretation. Second, the paper’s reliance on a curated set of multiple prompts per dataset raises concerns about sensitivity to prompt wording; although the authors test robustness, the degree to which prompt diversity alone drives performance (vs. model scale or architecture) remains unclear. Third, the comparison to GPT-3 is informative but confounded by architectural differences (encoder-decoder vs. decoder-only) and training objectives, leaving open whether the gains stem from multitask training per se or from better alignment of input-output formats. Finally, the author list contains a duplicate entry (Stephen H. Bach twice) and formatting inconsistencies, which, while minor, reflect on editorial care. Overall, the paper makes a strong empirical contribution but would benefit from a deeper discussion of what constitutes a “task” in this framework and from additional ablations to isolate the effect of explicit multitask supervision.

comments.title: Missing verb in model description
comments.quote: At a high level, we a pretrained model on our multi-task training mixture of natural language prompted datasets.
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically incomplete: 'we a pretrained model' lacks a main verb. The intended meaning is almost certainly 'we fine-tune a pretrained model' or 'we train a pretrained model', as clarified later in the same paragraph ('All models we trained are based on T5...'). This appears to be an OCR error where a verb was dropped. While the context resolves the intended action, the incomplete sentence could momentarily confuse a reader or introduce ambiguity about the exact training procedure (e.g., whether initialization involved further pretraining or only supervised fine-tuning). The later explicit statement that models are 'trained' on the mixture confirms the missing verb is fine-tuning, so this is a minor clarity issue rather than a substantive error.

comments.title: Unconventional notation for 2^20
comments.quote: corresponding to 2[20] total input tokens per batch
comments.explanation: The expression '2[20]' is nonstandard. Given that the batch size is 1024 sequences and each sequence is truncated to 1024 tokens, the total tokens per batch is 1024 × 1024 = 1,048,576 = 2²⁰. The brackets likely represent a superscript that was lost during OCR, producing '2[20]' instead of '2^{20}' or '2^20'. While a mathematically inclined reader can infer the intended value, the notation is ambiguous and could be misread as '2 times 20' = 40, which would be inconsistent with the batch size. This is a minor technical error because the correct interpretation is easily deduced from context.

comments.title: OCR corruption in variable name 'num_templates'
comments.quote: 500–9000 / num ~~t~~ emplates examples
comments.explanation: The text shows 'num ~~t~~ emplates' with strikethrough tildes around the 't'. This appears to be an OCR artifact where the intended variable name was likely 'num_templates' or 'num templates'. The surrounding explanation clarifies that the denominator is the number of templates created for the dataset, so the meaning is unambiguous. However, the corrupted form could confuse a reader who assumes the strikethrough is intentional. Since the correct interpretation is clear from context, this is a minor notation inconsistency rather than a substantive error.

comments.title: Underspecified number of prompts per dataset in ablation study
comments.quote: Including these prompts results in p being 8.03 on average (which corresponds to our main T0 model). We compare T0 to models where p = 1 (one randomly chosen original-task prompt per dataset), p = 5.7 on average (all original-tasks prompts for all datasets), and p = 0 (corresponding to T5+LM without any prompted training).
comments.explanation: The ablation study compares models with different average numbers of prompts per dataset (p). However, the number of datasets (d) is fixed but not explicitly stated. From context, 'fix d' refers to the main T0 model's 39 datasets (as per Figure 7). A careful reader would need to infer this from other figures and text. While the meaning is recoverable, the omission of the exact value of d in this passage could lead to confusion about the experimental setup, especially since Figure 6 is not provided in the excerpt. This is a mild underspecification.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about robustness to prompt formulation
comments.quote: These results suggest that T0 could be more robust to prompt formulation than GPT-3.
comments.explanation: The comparison is based on only one dataset (RTE) with 10 prompts. While the authors note that 9 of 10 prompts yield roughly random-guessing performance for GPT-3, this is a very narrow evaluation. Generalizing to 'more robust to prompt formulation' as a broad claim might overstate what has been shown. The authors moderate the claim with 'suggest' and 'could be', but the passage lacks a caveat about the limited scope (single dataset, one task type). A careful reader might incorrectly assume this robustness applies across all tasks. This is a mild overclaim/insufficient justification.

comments.title: Inconsistency in SuperGLUE held-out tasks description
comments.quote: lastly, T0++ further adds SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019a) to the training mixture (except RTE and CB), which leaves NLI and the BIG-bench tasks as the only held-out tasks.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, natural language inference (NLI) is defined as a held-out task, and RTE and CB are NLI datasets. The summary states that T0++ leaves only NLI and BIG-bench as held-out. However, the passage says SuperGLUE is added *except* RTE and CB, which are NLI datasets. This is consistent—the held-out NLI task still includes RTE and CB. However, a reader might initially think that excluding RTE and CB contradicts the earlier claim that NLI is held-out. Context resolves this: RTE and CB are part of SuperGLUE but are excluded from training to maintain NLI as held-out. No actual error, but the phrasing could be clarified to avoid momentary confusion.

comments.title: Missing model size for GPT-3 robustness comparison
comments.quote: Because Brown et al. (2020) only report one prompt per dataset with no standard deviation, we evaluate GPT-3 via OpenAI’s API[3] on RTE using the same 10 prompts we evaluate T0 in order to estimate GPT-3 robustness’ to different wording of prompts. One of these templates is identical to Brown et al. (2020, p. 59)’s reported prompt, which scores an accuracy of 58.8%, lower than the 63.5% reported in Brown et al. (2020).
comments.explanation: The passage does not specify which GPT-3 model size is used for the robustness test. The footnote references 'davinci' and estimates it corresponds to the 175B model, but the main text does not state the size. A reader comparing T0 (11B) to GPT-3 might wonder if the robustness test uses the 175B, 13B, or 6.7B model. The footnote resolves this partially, but the main text should be explicit for clarity.

comments.title: Possible ambiguity in 'all original-tasks prompts'
comments.quote: We compare T0 to models where p = 1 (one randomly chosen original-task prompt per dataset), p = 5.7 on average (all original-tasks prompts for all datasets), and p = 0 (corresponding to T5+LM without any prompted training).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'all original-tasks prompts' could be misinterpreted. It might mean all prompts that correspond to the dataset's original task (as opposed to non-original-task prompts like 'generate question from answer'). This is consistent with the preceding sentence, but a reader might wonder whether it includes non-original-task prompts or not. The later sentence clarifies that T0 includes *all* prompts (including non-original-task), so the p=5.7 model uses only original-task prompts. This is correct but could be stated more directly to avoid confusion.

comments.quote: Max Bartolo, Alastair Roberts, Johannes Welbl, Sebastian Riedel, and Pontus Stenetorp. Beat the ai: Investigating adversarial human annotation for reading comprehension. _Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics_ , 8:662–678, 2020. doi: 10.1162/tacl ~~a 0~~ 0338. URL https://doi.org/10.1162/tacl ~~a 0~~ 0338.
comments.explanation: The DOI is corrupted by OCR artifacts: '~~a 0~~' should likely be '_a_0' (i.e., 'tacl_a_0_0338'). The tilde-plus-space formatting is an OCR misread of an underscore. The correct DOI for this paper is 10.1162/tacl_a_0_0338. This is purely a formatting glitch and does not affect the scientific content, but it would prevent a reader from resolving the DOI directly.

The later sentence clarifies that T0 includes *all* prompts (including non-original-task), so the p=5.7 model uses only original-task prompts. This is correct but could be stated more directly to avoid confusion.

comments.title: OCR error in DOI for Bartolo et al. (2020)
comments.quote: Max Bartolo, Alastair Roberts, Johannes Welbl, Sebastian Riedel, and Pontus Stenetorp. Beat the ai: Investigating adversarial human annotation for reading comprehension. _Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics_ , 8:662–678, 2020. doi: 10.1162/tacl ~~a 0~~ 0338.
